gist: Cuba scholar Javier Corrales argues the Trump administration's January 2026 military intervention in Venezuela — which forced Maduro out and opened its oil and mining sectors to US investment within 24 hours — is being treated as a template for Cuba, but that Cuba's ideologically committed, embargo-profiting military makes a fast capitulation far less likely. He frames sanctions as generally failing to produce regime change while occasionally forcing policy reform, citing Cuba's surprise mid-June rollout of 176 market-oriented reforms.

insights.claim: In early January 2026 the US launched a surgical military operation in Venezuela that removed Nicolas Maduro and his wife, installed Maduro's number-two as interim president, and forced the regime to capitulate in under 24 hours on exactly the terms the US demanded.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest, political scientist, Amherst College)
insights.so_what: A regime-change playbook that works in a day resets geopolitical risk pricing for Latin American autocracies and their creditors; watch which regime the US targets next.
insights.quote: the United States essentially launched a war against Venezuela and in less than 24 hours, the regime capitulated

insights.claim: The US demanded no democratization from Venezuela and instead extracted two concrete concessions: opening the oil and mining sectors to US investment, and halting Venezuelan oil sales to Cuba.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Signals the intervention was resource-access driven, opening Venezuelan oil and mining to US capital while cutting a key energy lifeline to Cuba — directly tradable for energy and commodity exposure.
insights.quote: It simply demanded that the country allowed US investments in its oil and mining sector

insights.claim: Because the Trump administration views the Venezuela operation as a resounding success, it may be tempted to replicate it against Cuba, treating Cuba as an easy security problem to solve.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Escalation risk toward Cuba is rising; a Cuba intervention would carry higher execution risk than Venezuela given regime durability.

insights.claim: Venezuela's post-intervention outcome delivered foreign-policy and oil-access concessions but no market-oriented reforms, no democratization, and no end of the dictatorship, so a durable investment climate there remains unproven.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Access to Venezuelan oil and mining may not come with rule-of-law reforms, keeping expropriation and instability risk elevated for entrants.

insights.claim: US-Cuba relations are at one of their highest tension points since the end of the Cold War, with the Trump administration treating Cuba as both a genuine US security problem and an easy one to solve.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Rising bilateral tension raises the probability of a coercive US move against Cuba, a tail risk for Caribbean stability.

insights.claim: The second Trump administration has significantly tightened Cuba sanctions, including an effective oil blockade producing an energy crisis, an unsealed indictment against Raul Castro, and new restrictions on state-owned Cuban firms.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: An oil blockade and deepening energy crisis heighten the chance of destabilization or forced concessions in Cuba.
insights.quote: There has been, for example, now basically an oil blockade to Cuba which is producing an energy crisis

insights.claim: The Trump administration is pursuing a 'greater North American area of influence' doctrine seeking hegemony from the North Pole — potentially including Greenland — down to the equator, with Cuba and Nicaragua seen as the remaining holdouts.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: A stated hemispheric hegemony doctrine frames a multi-country pressure campaign, useful for anticipating where US coercion lands next.
insights.quote: this is an area that would run, you know, from the North Pole, maybe including Greenland, going all the way down to the equator

insights.claim: Corrales argues that modern autocracies survive sanctions by transferring costs onto their populations and building illicit trade networks, so economic pressure that fails to kill a regime quickly tends to entrench hardliners and weaken civil society.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: A core framework for pricing sanctions risk: they often strengthen targeted regimes rather than break them, undercutting bets on sanctions-driven regime collapse.
insights.quote: sanctions and economic pressures on dictatorships in the current world that we're in if they don't kill you right away, they make you stronger

insights.claim: Sanctions produce regime change mainly when the targeted government wants good relations with the West — as in 1980s apartheid South Africa — which is absent in Cuba where the regime has no interest in Western relations.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Screens which sanctioned regimes are likely to fold: those seeking Western reintegration, not defiant autarkic ones.

insights.claim: Even when sanctions fail to topple a regime, they can push a bad regime to change specific policies for the better, which is where the current scholarly debate is focused.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Reframes sanctions as a policy-leverage tool rather than a regime-change tool, relevant to modeling incremental concessions.

insights.claim: Cuba is cited as the clearest case of regime longevity despite 60-plus years of US sanctions since 1960, meaning bets on imminent sanctions-driven Cuban collapse have consistently failed.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Historical base rate argues against pricing in a near-term Cuban regime collapse from economic pressure alone.
insights.quote: Cuba is the best example of the longevity of a regime despite 60 years of US efforts to weaken the regimes through sanctions

themes.theme: Why Cuba's regime is harder to crack than Venezuela's

insights.claim: Cuba's state is run by committed ideologues across three loyalty-based branches — Fidel-era revolutionaries, the party, and the military — unlike Venezuela's pay-to-play elite where corrupt actors bought their way into power.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Ideologically committed elites are far less bribable or coercible than corrupt ones, lowering the odds of a Venezuela-style quick capitulation.

insights.claim: The Cuban military has profited heavily from the US embargo since 1990 and is anesthetized to US animosity, whereas Venezuela's elite only felt sanctions from 2019 and was desperate for a US bailout.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: A regime that monetizes its own embargo has little incentive to fold under more sanctions — a key reason Cuba won't mirror Venezuela.
insights.quote: the military makes a lot of money from the embargo. A lot of money. And they have been able to do this since 1990

insights.claim: Cuba's military is far more professional and battle-experienced than Venezuela's — having fought wars in Africa and trained in the Soviet bloc — and Venezuela had even relied on Cuban forces to protect its own regime.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Higher Cuban military capacity raises the cost and uncertainty of any US intervention, though Corrales notes they may be rusty.

insights.claim: Anti-Americanism in Cuba is confined to the ruling elite and absent in the general population, which craves American goods and made Obama wildly popular during 2014-2016 normalization.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: A pro-American public suggests large latent consumer and tourism demand if the regime opens, but also that popular discontent is aimed at the regime, not the US.

insights.claim: In mid-June 2026 Cuba announced 176 new market-oriented reforms that Corrales calls, on paper, the most drastic and significant he has seen in a long time, with debate over whether they respond to anticipated US pressure.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: A large reform package is a potential inflection point for Cuban economic opening and a signal that US pressure may be extracting policy change even without regime change.
insights.quote: by mid-June, they announced 176 new reforms, market-oriented reforms that I've looked at them, they are probably in writing, you know, on paper, they are the most drastic, the most significant reforms that I have seen in the long, long time in Cuba

insights.claim: A credible camp argues US sanctions on Cuba have historically been soft — full of loopholes, allowing remittances, and never a full blockade — so their apparent failure does not prove sanctions cannot work if fully enforced.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: Implies the current oil-blockade-level tightening is a genuinely new, untested level of pressure whose effects are not yet priced in.

insights.claim: Corrales prescribes a three-front approach to Cuba — economic liberalization, technocratic reform of the state, and genuine democratization with alternation in office — warning that the US appears focused only on the economic piece.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: A US approach limited to economic opening risks an incomplete transition that leaves the regime intact, capping the durability of any market opening.

insights.claim: Corrales argues incentivizing Cuba's elites to step down requires an international security guarantee that departure will not mean their elimination, but that any such shield must not leave the ruling elite and state intact.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: A negotiated soft-landing for elites is the mechanism most likely to unlock a peaceful transition, informing scenarios beyond pure coercion.

insights.claim: Unlike Venezuela, Cuba has no coherent democratic opposition or viable parties today, only small weak organizations, though Corrales believes leaders and parties could spring up within 7-8 months under the right legal environment.
insights.speaker: Javier Corrales (guest)
insights.so_what: The absence of a ready governing alternative raises post-regime-change chaos risk, a caution for any transition-timing thesis.
